questions.specification:
Morales and Jensen (2020) investigated how stigma affects willingness to seek treatment for depression. Their aim was to examine whether negative attitudes toward therapy predicted lower treatment-seeking behaviour.

A sample of 400 university students completed anonymous surveys about their attitudes toward mental health treatment and whether they had ever sought therapy. Questions assessed perceived stigma (“Most people think less of someone who goes to therapy”) and personal stigma (“I would feel embarrassed if I sought therapy”).

Results showed that high stigma—especially personal stigma—was strongly associated with lower willingness to seek treatment. 

[Note: Fictionalised but based on Corrigan’s stigma research.]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, perspective, responsibility, change.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Self-report subject to social desirability; sample limited to students.
Perspective: University students’ stigma may differ from older or clinical populations.
Responsibility: Researching stigma requires sensitivity; risk of reinforcing stereotypes.
Change: High stigma linked to lower treatment-seeking—interventions could reduce stigma over time.

questions.specification:
Chen and Alvarez (2015) investigated how children develop theory of mind across cultures. Their aim was to examine whether cultural differences in communication norms affect the age at which children succeed in false-belief tasks.

The study involved 200 children aged 4–8 from two countries: Country A, where parents encouraged early discussion of thoughts and feelings, and Country B, where such discussions were less common. Children were given a standard false-belief test, such as the “Sally-Anne” task, in which they had to predict where a character would search for an object that had been moved without their knowledge.

Results showed that children in Country A typically passed the task by age 5, while children in Country B often did not succeed until age 7 or 8. Researchers concluded that cultural norms around discussing mental states influence the developmental timeline of theory of mind. 

[Note: Fictionalised but inspired by cross-cultural research into theory of mind development, e.g., Wellman et al. (2001).]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, change, measurement, perspective.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: False-belief tasks culturally unfamiliar in one country.
Change: Developmental timeline differs across cultures—illustrates plasticity.
Measurement: Validity issues with tasks; standardised tests may not suit all cultures.
Perspective: Cultural context shapes cognitive development interpretation.

questions.specification:
Rodriguez and Leung (2016) studied whether different revision strategies influence long-term academic performance. The aim was to compare the effectiveness of spaced practice versus cramming in preparing for exams.

The sample included 120 university students randomly assigned to two conditions. In the spaced practice condition, students studied course material in weekly sessions across six weeks. In the cramming condition, students studied the same material for an equivalent total time but in a single long session one day before the exam. Performance was assessed through two tests: one taken immediately after the study period, and another delayed test six weeks later.

Results showed that while both groups performed similarly on the immediate test, students in the spaced practice group scored significantly higher on the delayed test, suggesting better retention. Researchers noted that some students in the spaced condition dropped out due to time constraints.

[Note: Fictionalised but based on empirical findings in cognitive psychology on distributed vs. massed practice (e.g., Cepeda et al., 2006).]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, causality, change, measurement.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Dropouts in spaced condition; stress levels uncontrolled.
Causality: Random assignment increases cause-effect confidence.
Change: Long-term retention illustrates cognitive change over time.
Measurement: Exam scores valid outcome but influenced by external factors.

questions.specification:
Kumar and Delgado (2019) examined predictors of divorce in a 10-year longitudinal study of married couples. Their aim was to identify whether communication patterns and stress regulation during conflicts predicted long-term relationship outcomes.

The study followed 150 couples from early marriage over a decade. Every two years, couples completed questionnaires about marital satisfaction and stress levels. In addition, they participated in videotaped conflict-resolution tasks where they discussed ongoing disagreements while researchers coded their interactions for hostility, defensiveness, and problem-solving strategies. Salivary cortisol samples were collected to measure physiological stress.

Results indicated that couples who displayed high levels of hostility and poor stress regulation during conflict were significantly more likely to divorce within ten years. By contrast, couples who used positive communication strategies and showed stable cortisol levels were more likely to remain married. Researchers noted some attrition; many couples dropped out.

[Note: Fictionalised but draws on Gottman’s research.]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, causality, change, responsibility.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Couples who dropped out skew results; survivors may not represent divorcing couples.
Causality: Conflict observed predicts divorce, but other factors (e.g., finances) unmeasured.
Change: Communication patterns evolve—snapshot coding may miss long-term shifts.
Responsibility: Recording intimate conflicts raises privacy issues; stress during tasks may harm couples.

parts.content: To what extent does change explain research on schema development in the context of human development?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of change or schema development.
Mentions “schemas” without accuracy.
Minimal or irrelevant examples.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of change in schema research.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Piaget’s stages, Bartlett’s “War of the Ghosts”).
Explains how schemas adapt/change with development.
Some evaluation (cultural variation, methodological issues).
Partial conclusion.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of schema change and development.
Multiple studies (Bartlett, Piaget, Anderson & Pichert).
Well-developed analysis of schema change across age/culture.
Balanced evaluation (support for developmental change, but limitations in methodology/generalizability).
Accurate terminology, well-reasoned conclusion.

parts.content: To what extent does change explain cultural influences on relationship formation in the context of human relationships?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of change or cultural influences on relationships.
Mentions collectivism/individualism vaguely.
Little or no relevant research used.
Response is descriptive, lacks evaluation.
Psychological terminology is inaccurate or inconsistently applied.
**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of cultural influences on relationships.
Refers to at least one relevant study (e.g., Gupta & Singh on arranged vs love marriages, Dion & Dion on cultural norms in partner choice).
Explains change in cultural practices or expectations in relationship formation.
Some evaluation of cultural bias or methodological limitations.
Conclusion present but not fully developed.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of change in cultural influences on relationships.
Accurate use of multiple studies (Gupta & Singh, Dion & Dion, Buss on mate preferences across cultures).
Explains how globalization, acculturation, or modernization create cultural change in partner selection and relationship norms.
Well-developed evaluation of strengths and limitations, with cross-cultural comparisons.
Balanced and reasoned conclusion linking change, cultural influences, and relationship formation.
Accurate and precise use of psychological terminology throughout.

questions.specification:
Andersson and Patel (2014) conducted a longitudinal study to investigate how attachment in infancy relates to later romantic relationships. The researchers were particularly interested in whether early secure attachment predicts higher relationship satisfaction in adulthood.

The original sample consisted of 200 infants assessed for attachment style at 12 months using the “Strange Situation” procedure. At age 25, participants who remained in the study (N = 92) were interviewed about their romantic relationships. Semi-structured interviews asked participants about satisfaction, trust, and conflict management.

Results showed that individuals classified as securely attached in infancy were significantly more likely to report positive and stable relationships at age 25 compared to those classified as insecurely attached. However, the researchers noted a large proportion of participants dropped out.

[Note: Fictionalised but strongly based on Hazan & Shaver’s research.]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, change, measurement, perspective. (Context: Human Development)
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Dropouts = attrition bias. Semi-structured interviews risk interviewer effects.
Change: Later experiences can alter attachment; not deterministic.
Measurement: Strange Situation artificial; adult interviews subjective.
Perspective: Western emphasis on romance—may not apply across cultures.

questions.specification:
Tanaka and Johnson (2016) tested whether a new therapy program reduced anxiety in post-surgical patients. The aim was to compare its effectiveness against standard care and to evaluate both self-reported and physiological indicators of anxiety.

Patients recovering from surgery (N = 60) were randomly assigned to receive either the therapy or standard care. The therapy consisted of six one-hour sessions that included relaxation training, guided imagery, and coping strategies. Anxiety was assessed using both self-report questionnaires and physiological measures, such as heart rate and blood pressure.

Results showed that patients in the therapy group reported lower anxiety and also demonstrated reduced heart rates compared to the control group. However, some patients reported discomfort with probing therapy questions, and others dropped out. The researchers concluded that the therapy showed promise.

[Note: Fictionalised but consistent with real anxiety intervention studies.]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, change, measurement, responsibility.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Teacher reports of “integration” subjective, risk of stereotyping. Word lists may have cultural bias.
Change: Memory strategies shifted as children integrated—illustrates adaptation over time.
Measurement: Free recall tasks may not reflect real-world learning; low ecological validity.
Responsibility: Testing newly arrived children could be stressful—researchers must balance insight with protection.

parts.content: To what extent does the concept of change explain the effectiveness of biological treatments in the context of health & well-being?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited knowledge of change or biological treatments.
Mentions medication vaguely (e.g., antidepressants).
Weak or inaccurate links to health & well-being.
Minimal evaluation.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of change and biological treatments.
Refers to at least one treatment (e.g., SSRIs for depression, antipsychotics for schizophrenia).
Explains how treatments produce change (e.g., serotonin reuptake).
Some evaluation of effectiveness (e.g., Kirsch’s placebo findings).
Partial conclusion.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of change in biological treatments.
Multiple treatments/studies explained (SSRIs: Kirsch et al., Leucht on antipsychotics, ECT).
Well-developed evaluation of effectiveness (short-term vs. long-term, side effects, placebo effects, ethics).
Clear links to health & well-being outcomes (quality of life, symptom reduction).
Accurate terminology, reasoned conclusion.

parts.content: To what extent does the concept of change explain cultural influences on personal relationships in the context of human relationships?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited knowledge of change or cultural influences on personal relationships.
Mentions “relationships change over time” vaguely, without clear cultural reference.
Minimal or no use of relevant research.
Weak or no link to human relationships.
Psychological terminology inaccurate or inconsistently applied.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate understanding of change in cultural influences on relationships.
At least one relevant study explained (e.g., Gupta & Singh on arranged vs. love marriages; Dion & Dion on cultural norms; Levine et al. on cultural attitudes to love).
Explains change in terms of modernization, acculturation, or globalization influencing formation/maintenance.
Some evaluation of cultural research (e.g., cultural bias, ecological validity).
Conclusion present but underdeveloped.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong understanding of change in cultural influences on personal relationships.
Multiple studies integrated (Gupta & Singh, Dion & Dion, Buss on mate preferences, Levine et al.).
Clear explanation of change:
Globalization alters cultural norms about partner choice and relationship expectations.
Acculturation shapes how immigrant populations adapt relationship practices.
Gender role changes across cultures influence formation and maintenance.
Well-developed evaluation: strengths (cross-cultural comparisons show cultural diversity and universals), limitations (risk of ethnocentrism, self-report bias, overgeneralisation).
Explicit link to human relationships: cultural change provides insight into why relationships are formed and maintained differently across societies.
Balanced conclusion: change is key, but structural/economic factors also play roles.

parts.content: To what extent does research on neuroplasticity demonstrate change in the context of learning & cognition?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Demonstrates limited knowledge of neuroplasticity or the concept of change.
Descriptions of studies (if included) are very general, inaccurate, or irrelevant.
Little or no attempt to link neuroplasticity to learning & cognition.
Response is descriptive, with minimal analysis or evaluation.
Psychological terminology is absent, inaccurate, or used inconsistently.
Any conclusion included is very superficial or not related to the argument.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Shows adequate knowledge and understanding of neuroplasticity and the concept of change.
Explains at least one relevant study (e.g., Draganski et al. 2004 on juggling, Maguire 2000 on taxi drivers). Description is mostly accurate but may lack detail.
Links to learning & cognition are attempted and partly developed.
Includes some analysis or evaluation (e.g., neuroplasticity demonstrates structural brain change due to experience) but lacks depth or balance.
Psychological terminology is used, sometimes appropriately, though imprecisely.
A conclusion is present but not fully consistent with the arguments presented.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Demonstrates strong knowledge and understanding of neuroplasticity and change.
Explains and uses two or more relevant studies accurately (e.g., Draganski et al., Maguire et al., Rosenzweig & Bennett).
Provides well-developed analysis of how neuroplasticity demonstrates change in relation to learning & cognition (e.g., experience-dependent plasticity, synaptic pruning, structural vs. functional plasticity).
Critically evaluates the research (e.g., methodological strengths/limitations, issues of ecological validity, animal vs. human studies).
Uses psychological terminology accurately and precisely.
Draws a reasoned, balanced conclusion that is consistent with the arguments presented (e.g., neuroplasticity demonstrates change clearly, but evidence varies in scope and generalizability).

questions.specification:
Almeida and Cho (2017) conducted a field study to explore bystander behaviour in public spaces. Their aim was to examine whether people were more likely to help when alone or in groups, and whether cultural background influenced this tendency.

Confederates staged minor emergencies—such as dropping a stack of books or stumbling on a curb—in busy pedestrian areas. Researchers observed 500 incidents across two cities: one in a collectivist culture and one in an individualist culture. They recorded whether passersby offered help and how quickly they responded.

Findings showed that individuals who were alone were quicker to help than those in groups, supporting the “diffusion of responsibility” explanation. Cultural differences were also evident: people in the collectivist city helped more frequently overall, even in group contexts. 

[Note: Fictionalised but reflects Darley & Latané’s bystander research]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, causality, change, perspective.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Observational study lacks control; cultural stereotypes possible.
Causality: Field setting weakens cause-effect certainty.
Change: Group vs. alone conditions illustrate situational change in behaviour.
Perspective: Diffusion of responsibility interpreted differently across collectivist/individualist contexts.

parts.content: To what extent does the concept of change explain acculturation outcomes in the context of human development?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited knowledge of change or acculturation.
Mentions adaptation or culture shock vaguely without depth.
Response is descriptive with minimal or inaccurate reference to development.
Little or no evaluation. Terminology is weak.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of change in acculturation.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Berry’s acculturation model, Lueck & Wilson on acculturative stress).
Explains how change occurs in cultural identity, stress levels, or adaptation.
Some evaluation of generalizability or cultural variation.
Conclusion present but underdeveloped.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong understanding of change in acculturation outcomes.
Accurate use of multiple studies (Berry, Lueck & Wilson, Miranda & Matheny).
Explains mechanisms of change (integration, assimilation, marginalization, separation).
Well-developed evaluation of methods, strengths, and limitations.
Accurate terminology, balanced and reasoned conclusion.
